mission.title: Install and Configure Oauth2 Proxy on Kubernetes
mission.description: OAuth2 Proxy is a flexible, open-source tool that acts as a reverse proxy providing authentication with various OAuth2 and OIDC providers. Installing it allows you to secure your web applications easily with OAuth2 authentication.

steps.title: Add the OAuth2 Proxy Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the official OAuth2 Proxy Helm repository to your Helm client:
```bash
helm repo add oauth2-proxy https://oauth2-proxy.github.io/manifests
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install OAuth2 Proxy using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install OAuth2 Proxy with the specified version in a new namespace:
```bash
helm install oauth2-proxy oauth2-proxy/oauth2-proxy --version 7.14.3 --namespace oauth2-proxy --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the OAuth2 Proxy pod is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace oauth2-proxy
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure your OAuth2 Proxy has adequate resources, apply resource limits:
```bash
kubectl set resources deployment/oauth2-proxy --namespace oauth2-proxy --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access the service
steps.description:
To access the OAuth2 Proxy service locally, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/oauth2-proxy --namespace oauth2-proxy 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the OAuth2 Proxy pod running in the 'oauth2-proxy' namespace, and you will be able to access it via port 8080 on your local machine. You can further configure it to suit your authentication needs.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add oauth2-proxy https://oauth2-proxy.github.io/manifests
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the OAuth2 Proxy release, use the following command to uninstall it from the specified namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall oauth2-proxy --namespace oauth2-proxy
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If there are any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) or persistent resources that were created during the installation, you can remove them with the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd oauth2proxies.oauth2-proxy.github.com
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace oauth2-proxy
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, remove the namespace if it is no longer needed and verify that it has been deleted:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace oauth2-proxy
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it is a good practice to back up the current configuration and state of the OAuth2 Proxy. You can do this by exporting the current values:
```bash
helm get values oauth2-proxy --namespace oauth2-proxy > oauth2-proxy-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and then upgrade the OAuth2 Proxy to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade oauth2-proxy oauth2-proxy/oauth2-proxy --version 7.15.0 --namespace oauth2-proxy
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the OAuth2 Proxy pod is running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace oauth2-proxy
kubectl describe deployment oauth2-proxy --namespace oauth2-proxy | grep Image
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the OAuth2 Proxy pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace oauth2-proxy
```
Look for configuration issues or missing environment variables.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the service is not reachable, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. You can check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace oauth2-proxy
```
Verify that the correct ports are being forwarded.

troubleshooting.title: Authentication errors
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter authentication errors, check the OAuth2 Proxy configuration. Review the logs for any authentication-related messages:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace oauth2-proxy | grep 'authentication'
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing pod eviction
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are being evicted due to resource limits, check the events in the namespace:
```bash
kubectl get events --namespace oauth2-proxy
```
Consider adjusting the resource limits set for the deployment.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and Helm installed to deploy OAuth2 Proxy.
